one_line_takeaway: A governance-oriented PRISMA survey of Responsible GenAI that contributes a ten-criterion rubric scoring ~20 AI-safety benchmarks (top: Rainbow Teaming 0.50) plus twelve lifecycle KPIs, showing safety-benchmark coverage is dense for bias/toxicity but sparse for privacy, deepfakes, and agentic system-level failures.
key_finding: Three findings emerge from the rubric-scored comparison: (1) benchmark coverage is dense for bias (C1) and toxicity (C2) but sparse for privacy (C6), provenance/deepfakes (C5), and system-level failures in agentic settings (C7) — e.g., deepfake integrity is covered notably only by SHIELD and system-failure probing appears mainly in Rainbow Teaming; (2) evaluations remain largely static and task-local, limiting audit portability across releases; and (3) inconsistent documentation complicates cross-release comparison. On the normalized rubric, Rainbow Teaming scores highest at 0.50 (strong adaptive/red-team security coverage), while single-issue suites like RealToxicityPrompts and TrustGPT score 0.15; no benchmark approaches full coverage, echoing the cited MIT AI Risk Repository finding that current safety frameworks cover only ~34% of governance-body-identified risks. The authors stress the scores measure breadth of governance-ready evidence, not overall safety rankings.
core_contribution: A PRISMA-guided systematic survey of Responsible Generative AI (ResGenAI) that the authors position as the first to simultaneously bridge governance principles, technical evaluation, and domain deployment across all four GenAI system types (LLMs, VLMs, diffusion models, agentic pipelines). Its four stated own contributions are: (1) a cross-cutting survey spanning CS theory, application, policy, and GenAI (the 'Ours' row of Table 1, claimed to be the only survey covering all four thematic areas and all four novelty dimensions); (2) a ten-criterion rubric (C1 Bias/Discrimination, C2 Toxicity, C3 Security/Robustness, C4 Misinformation, C5 Deepfakes/Media Integrity, C6 Privacy, C7 System-level Failure, C8 Malicious-actor Realism, C9 Metric Validity/Documentation, C10 Governance Alignment) that scores widely used AI safety benchmarks on risk-surface coverage, plus a policy crosswalk (Table 5) mapping benchmarks to NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act requirements; (3) twelve lifecycle KPIs with explicit formulas (data quality Q, privacy compliance P, SPD, DI, accuracy parity, robustness/ASR, explainability index E(x), local faithfulness gap LFG, high-stakes error rate E_h, audit frequency/resolution, user inclusivity U, energy/emissions), foundation-model explainability guidance, and an AI-ready testbed catalogue (Table 6) plus an 'Accountability Matrix' (Table 7) linking KPIs to benchmarks and audit toolkits; and (4) a domain analysis across healthcare, finance, education, arts, agriculture, and defense mapping each sector to salient rubric criteria and priority KPIs. It is a synthesis/framework paper, not a new attack, defense, or empirical system.
threat_model: Being a survey, the paper does not propose a defense against a single adversary; instead it catalogues the ResGenAI risk landscape it reviews. Established technical risks include hallucination/unverifiability, membership inference and verbatim training-data extraction, bias/representational harm, and calibration failure. Emergent 'second-generation' threats it names include text-embedding inversion that reconstructs training data (defeating anonymization), gradient-optimized universal adversarial jailbreak suffixes, prompt injection, 'shadow AI' (guardrail-stripped fine-tunes outside oversight), and 'synthetic identity swarms' generating synthetic-consensus cascades. Agentic-specific failure vectors (Table 2) are tool misuse / unauthorized API calls, recursive hallucination in planning, authentication/credential leakage during tool calls, and plan hijacking via chain-of-thought manipulation. A recurring meta-threat is 'safetywashing': models scoring high on static keyword-driven refusal benchmarks (e.g., SafetyBench) while failing under multi-turn, adversarial, socially situated interaction.
limitations: Self-acknowledged: the assessment is a subjective ordinal expert appraisal (0/1/2 by two reviewers), explicitly not a quantitative meta-analysis with effect sizes, and the scores 'should not be interpreted as rankings of overall safety.' Scoring rests on heterogeneous public documentation, so coverage judgments may be contestable. No new empirical experiments are run — headline quantitative claims about model behavior (e.g., the GPT-4 1.47% clinical hallucination rate on 450 transcripts, Chatbot Arena's 800k votes, AIF360's 70+ metrics) are drawn from cited third-party work, not the authors' own measurements. There is an unreconciled internal inconsistency in the corpus size (abstract: 232 studies; Methodology/PRISMA flow: 142). The rubric applies to a curated set of ~20 benchmarks in Table 3, not exhaustively. As a survey it offers frameworks and recommendations (rubric, KPIs, audit loop) that are proposed but not empirically validated in deployment.

datasets_used: Literature corpus: 5,313 records retrieved from 5 databases (Scopus 1502, Web of Science 2798, IEEE Xplore 279, ACM DL 656, arXiv 78); 142 studies in final synthesis (abstract inconsistently says 232)

AI safety benchmarks assessed under the C1-C10 rubric (Table 3): Rainbow Teaming, Risk Taxonomy, HumaniBench, DecodingTrust, MLCommons AI Safety v0.5, SALAD-Bench, ALERT, MM-SafetyBench, HELM, TrustLLM, HarmBench, SafetyBench, XSTest, SHIELD, BIG-bench, PrivLM-Bench, MFG for GenAI, PrivacyLens, RealToxicityPrompts, TrustGPT
No new primary dataset is collected; the paper synthesizes existing benchmark documentation, model/dataset cards, and public leaderboards
quantitative_results: Rubric breadth scores S(B) in [0,1] (Table 3): Rainbow Teaming 0.50 (highest), Risk Taxonomy 0.45, HumaniBench 0.40, DecodingTrust 0.40, MLCommons AI Safety v0.5 0.35, SALAD-Bench 0.35, ALERT 0.35, MM-SafetyBench 0.35, HELM 0.30, TrustLLM 0.30, HarmBench 0.25, SafetyBench 0.25, XSTest 0.25, SHIELD 0.25, BIG-bench 0.25, PrivLM-Bench 0.20, MFG for GenAI 0.20, PrivacyLens 0.20, RealToxicityPrompts 0.15, TrustGPT 0.15
Weighting sensitivity analysis: Spearman rank correlation rho > 0.85 between equal-weight and high-stakes-weighted (C3/C6) profiles

PRISMA funnel: 5,313 initial -> 2,125 dedup removed -> 609+53 ineligible removed -> 2,526 screened -> 2,117 excluded at title/abstract -> 409 full-text sought (37 unretrievable) -> 372 assessed -> 230 excluded -> 142 final synthesis set

Cited context stats (not the authors' own experiments): AI safety frameworks cover only ~34% of governance-body-identified risks (MIT AI Risk Repository); 74% of organizations use GenAI but only 26% have a comprehensive RAI strategy; GPT-4 on 450 consultation transcripts showed 1.47% hallucination rate with 44% of errors major (ref [102]); systematic review of 778 studies on XAI and clinician trust

Twelve lifecycle KPIs defined with formulas, e.g., data quality Q = valid/total x100, SPD = P(y=1|A=a) - P(y=1|A=b), DI ratio, robustness R and ASR, high-stakes error E_h, energy E_kWh and emissions C_CO2e = E_kWh x PUE x CI
baselines_compared: Prior surveys compared in Table 1 by thematic coverage (CS Theory/Application/Policy/GenAI) and novelty dimensions (Taxonomy/Scope/Evaluation/Methods); the 'Ours' 2026 row claims full coverage on all eight columns where prior surveys have gaps

Closest evaluation-taxonomy / trust-assessment surveys [48] Trustworthy LLMs (ACM CSUR 2025) and [108] LLM Safety Evaluation (arXiv 2025), which the authors say partially address evaluation taxonomy but do not link benchmarks to regulatory audit-evidence needs
Other positioned-against surveys include [218] Trustworthy LLM Agents (KDD 2025), [40] LLM Safeguards, [65] LLM Agent Security, [211] Reasoning Model Safety, and [190] MIT AI Risk Repository
Governance instruments used as external reference frames: NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, OECD Principles (Tables 4-5)
